coredata.dc:title: RETRACTED: A fluorescent molecularly-imprinted polymer gate with temperature and pH as inputs for detection of alpha-fetoprotein
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Karfa, Paramita
Roy, Ekta
Patra, Santanu
Kumar, Deepak
Madhuri, Rashmi
Sharma, Prashant K.
coredata.dc:description: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/our-business/policies/article-withdrawal). This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor following concerns raised by a reader. The article reuses an electron micrograph from a previous publication while claiming that these are different particles. Fig. 1C/D were reused from Fig. 2B published in Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Volume 73, 15 November 2015, Pages 234–244, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2015.06.005. This problem with the data presented casts doubt on all the data, and accordingly also the conclusions based on that data, in this publication. As such this article represents a severe abuse of the scientific publishing system. The scientific community takes a very strong view on this matter and apologies are offered to readers of the journal that this was not detected during the submission process.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ly fe k 4, In 12, I Sch Available online 2 December 2015 rte el s P is rosi The synthesized AFP-imprinted polymer possesses excellent selectivity towards their template molecule and s ving t In the based functional monomer and caffeine as a template. Till date, responsive imprinted polymers including magnetic (Shi, 2009), le structure and changes in tem- H, temperatures, number of func- unctional mono- rinting the small e of components s-linker, initiator le and stable in organic solvent, whereas biomolecules such as proteins need to be tein imprinting stimuli responsive MIPs are the best platform to Contents lists available at ScienceDirect .e Biosensors and Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2015.11.092photonic (Fang et al., 2012), temperature (Ma et al., 2012), and pH imprint macromolecules. Although, the work reported so far on the protein or biomolecule imprinting in responsive polymer matrix is very few. In this work, we have tried to synthesize dual stimuli 0956-5663/& 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. n Corresponding author. E-mail address: rshmmadhuri@gmail.com (R. Madhuri).several works have been reported for single and multi-stimuli imprinted under natural environments conditions to maintain their conformational integrity. Based on the requirements of pro-known that biological receptors can able to exhibit outstanding molecular recognition property in the aqueous environments and responsive behavior in the presence of external stimuli like tem- perature and pH change. Therefore, to mimic the biological re- ceptors, some researchers have started the preparation of biomi- metic MIPs with stimuli-responsive template binding properties (SR-MIPs). In the year 2006, Gong et al. has reported that with a suitable choice of functional monomers, MIPs can be switched by externally applied stimuli should be possible (Gong et al., 2006). They have synthesized a photo-responsive MIP using azobenzene- stream MIP technology where organic solvents w polymer preparation; (2) proteins have a ï¬‚exib conformation, which can be easily affected by perature or in the environment like high/low p salt concentrations etc.; (3) proteins have large tional groups available for the interaction with f mers. The fundamental difference between imp molecules and large biomolecules is in the choic involved in the polymerization i.e. monomer, cros and porogens. Small molecules are usually solubRE Tcompare the properties of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)with natural receptors like enzyme, antibodies etc. and it is also romolecule imprinting is difï¬�cult because: (1) proteins are water-soluble compounds that are not always compatible with main-ere used for the Stimuli-responsive polymer Fluorescence sensor Molecularly imprinted polymer Alpha-fetoprotein 1. Introduction Molecular imprinting is a ï¬‚exible generation of artiï¬�cial receptor ha sites for a speciï¬�c target molecule.logic gate with two stimuli (pH and temperature) as inputs. However, the non-imprinted polymers did not have such â€˜ORâ€™ gate property, which conï¬�rms the role of template binding. The imprinted polymer was also used for estimation of AFP in the concentration range of 3.96â€“80.0 ng mL 1, with limit of de- tection (LOD) 0.42 ng mL 1. The role of proposed sensor was successfully exploited for analysis of AFP in real human blood plasma, serum and urine sample. & 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. imple approach for the ailor-made recognition literature, we always (Zhao et al., 2009) responsive MIPs. Even though after the tremendous progress made in the ï¬�eld of SR-MIP, many big challenges still remain to be addressed in which the most popular one is biomolecule/macromolecule imprinting such as proteins, saccharides, DNAs, cells, and viruses. The mac- RA CKeywords:Molecular gate and dual-stimuli responsive behavior. Along with this, the imprinted polymer was also explored as â€˜ORâ€™A ï¬‚uorescent molecularly-imprinted po and pH as inputs for detection of alpha- Paramita Karfa a, Ekta Roy a, Santanu Patra a, Deepa Prashant K. Sharma c a Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, Jharkhand 82600 b Solid State and Structural Chemistry Unit, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 5600 c Functional Nanomaterials Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Physics, Indian a r t i c l e i n f o Article history: Received 27 July 2015 Received in revised form 28 November 2015 Accepted 30 November 2015 a b s t r a c t In this work, we have repo polymer (MIP) for trace lev The stimuli-responsive MI responsive component (ty journal homepage: wwwmer gate with temperature toprotein Kumar b, Rashmi Madhuri a,n, dia ndia ool of Mines, Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India d a new approach on the use of stimuli-responsive molecularly imprinted ensing of alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), which is a well know cancer biomarker. composed of three components, a thermo-responsive monomer, a pH ne derivative) and a highly ï¬‚uorescent vinyl silane modiï¬�ed carbon dot. lsevier.com/locate/bios Bioelectronics TE D In this work, we have used three different monomers, 2-acry- P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463 455responsive polymer matrix imprinted for Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), which is a potential biomarker for hepatocellular carcinoma dis- ease (Tothill, 2009). Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is an oncofetal protein of molecular weight 72 kDa, produced by normal gastrointestinal cells, yolk sac cells, and fetal hepatocytes, immediately after birth. Within 300 days, after birth, the serum AFP level decreases and reached to o10 ng mL 1 (Carlo et al., 2012). An elevated con- centration of AFP (420 ng mL 1) in serum is used as an early indication of hepatic carcinoma, including nasopharyngeal cancer and epithelial ovarian tumors (Malati, 2007). Detection of these biomarkers is challenging because they are present in very low concentration in the biological ï¬‚uid. In the literature, several capable detection methods based on the speciï¬�c recognition of AFP on the cancer cell surface/biological ï¬‚uid have been reported, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Teama et al., 2013), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Liu et al., 2013), electrophoresis (Zaninotto et al., 1996), surface plasmon resonance (SPR) (Teramura and Iwata, 2007), surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Wang et al., 2013), colorimetric assay (Wang et al., 2009), electrochemical immunoassay (Jiang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015), enzyme-immunoassay (Zhang et al., 2009), chemiluminescence (Yang et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2008) and ï¬‚uorescence methods (Xiang et al., 2012) etc. Among them the most popular one is sensors/biosensor which is developed by modiï¬�cation of transducer surface with natural or synthetic re- ceptors along with nanomaterials (Wang et al. 2014a, 2014b; Gao et al., 2013). Wang et al. (2014a) have developed a label-free im- munosensor based on Pd nanoplates for amperometric im- munoassay. Gao et al. (2013) have also developed a label-free electrochemical immunosensor by polyamidoamine dendrimers- capped carbon dots/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites as a base for detection of AFP. Recently, Wang et al. have reported a dyna beads- labeled magnetic immunoassay using an integrated giant magne- to-impedance biosensor for the detection of AFP in the range of 1.0â€“10.0 ng/ml. Other than the reported electrochemical im- munosensor some of the optical immunosensor were also re- ported for real sample analysis of AFP. Nie et al. have reported a CdSe nanoclusters/multiwall carbon nanotubes-based electro- chemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for AFP with detection limit of 0.4 pg mL 1 (Nie et al., 2014). Similarly, Xiang et al. (2012) have reported a microscale immunoassay by the incorporation of colorimetry and droplet platform. In this sensor ruthenium com- plex was coupled with the monoclonal antibody of AFP to form a stable red Ruâ€“Ab complex both as a quencher for green CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and as a capture probe for AFP. The literature reported so far for the detection of AFP is based on use of antibodies as natural recognition material which requires sophisticated handling or large analysis time and very costly. Therefore, Lee et al. (2014) have synthesized a synthetic receptor (MIP) based-nanoparticles to remove AFP from a from human hepatocellular carcinoma HepG2 cellular culture medium (Lee et al., 2014). Recently, Wang et al. (2015) have developed an electrochemical multiplexed immunoassay using recombinant apoferritin-encoded metallic nanoparticles and dual-template magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers as capture probes for trace level detection of AFP (Wang et al., 2015). In this paper, we report, for the ï¬�rst time, a highly selective and sensitive water-compatible, stimuli-responsive imprinted poly- mer-based ï¬‚uorescent molecular-gate for trace level sensing of AFP. Herein, the stimuli-responsive ï¬‚uorescent polymer matrix was synthesized, using three different functional monomer having speciï¬�c characteristics. The ï¬�rst monomer used in the synthesis is a glutamic acid derivative expected to provide pH-responsive be- havior to the polymer matrix. The well-known thermoresponsive monomer i.e. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) was used as another RE TR Amonomer. In addition, a vinylsilane modiï¬�ed carbon dotloylamino-pentanedioic acid (APA) as pH responsive precursor, NIPAm as thermo-responsive monomer and vinyl functionalized carbon dots as ï¬‚uorescent monomer (CDs). Their synthesis pro- cedures are given below: 2.2.1. Synthesis of glutamic acid derivative (APA) For the synthesis of glutamic acid derivative as monomer, 10.0 mL of 0.5 M acryloyl chloride was added into the 0.5 M cold, basic solution of glutamic acid (10.0 mL) and stirred for 1 h at 40 Â°C (Patra et al., 2015). After the completion of reaction, the mixture was acidiï¬�ed with 37% HCl (pHÂ¼2), extracted with ethyl acetate and stored in vacuum desiccator (yieldÂ¼87%). The com- pound was characterized on the basis of elemental analysis [cal- culated (%) for C8H11NO5: CÂ¼41.13, HÂ¼5.18, NÂ¼7.99; found (%): CÂ¼41.0, HÂ¼5.21, NÂ¼8.03] and FT-IR (cm 1): 3290 (Ï…â€“Oâ€“H, â€“(silane@CDs) was used to enhance the luminescence property of imprinted polymer matrix. To provide the water-compatibility, N, Nâ€²-methylene bisacrylamide and ammonium peroxodisulphate (APS) was used as cross-linker and initiator. In contrast to the other reported ï¬‚uorescence materials, the synthesized imprinted polymer has its own ï¬‚uorescent property, which gets enhanced after binding with template (AFP) molecule. The ï¬‚uorescence in- tensity shows a linear response with increase in concentration of AFP in the range of 3.96â€“80.0 ng mL 1, with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.42 ng mL 1. The binding of template to the MIP-cavities follow the â€˜ORâ€™ logic gate functioning with temperature and pH as the inputs. Along with this, the results obtained by the proposed sensor was also compared with the commercially available ELISA kits for AFP and found that the prepared MIP-sensor is much faster, sensitive and easy in use. 2. Experimental section 2.1. Reagent and instrumentation All chemicals were of analytical grade. Glutamic acid, IgG from rabbit serum (IgG), sucrose, N, Nâ€²-bisacrylamide, acryloyl chloride, NIPAm, ethyl acetate, lysine (Lys), histidine (His), globulin (Glo), albumin (Alb), bilirubin (Bil), urea, uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glu), sodium chloride (99.5%) and other chemicals were purchased from Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany) and Fluka (Steinheim, Germany). AFP (SKU-Pack SizeÂ¼25 mg with 495% purity) and Apolipoprotein C-I Human Recombinant (A1) has been purchased from ProSpec Tany Techno Gene LTD (USA). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium chloride (KCl), triethoxyvinyl silane, hydrochloric acid, dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), APS and petro- leum ether were procured from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Human blood serum, plasma, and urine samples were collected from local pathological laboratory (ISM, Dhanbad). The size and morphology of prepared silane@CDs was studied using a transmission electron microscope (model Tecnai 30 G 2 S-Twin electron microscope) operated at 300 KV accelerating voltages by placing a drop of as-prepared sample suspension on the surface of a carbon-coated copper grid. The morphology of the imprinted and non-imprinted polymers surfaces were recorded using ï¬�eld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), Zeiss, model Supra 55. The ï¬‚uorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer LS55 ï¬‚uorescence spectrophotometer and UVâ€“vis characterization was done by Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 (Singapore) spectrophotometer. 2.2. Synthesis of various functional monomers CT EDCOOH group), 1680 (Ï…â€“CÂ¼O), 1600 (Ï…â€“CÂ¼C), 1210 (Ï…â€“Câ€“Oâ€“, â€“ constant) were calculated from the slope and intercept of the linear plot of Q/C versus Q. P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463456 ACOOH group). The 1H and 13C NMR spectra were also recorded and given in Figs. S1 and S2. 2.2.2. Preparation of vinyl group functionalized carbon dot (Silane@CDs) The vinyl group functionalized carbon dots (Silane@CDs) were synthesized from anhydrous sucrose following an earlier reported method (Wang et al., 2011). In brief, 10.0 mL triethoxy vinyl silane was placed into a 100.0 mL three-necked ï¬‚ask, and degassed with nitrogen for about 10 min followed by heating the mixture to 240 Â°C. Then, 0.5 g anhydrous sucrose was quickly added to the ï¬‚ask with vigorous stirring and kept at the temperature for ad- ditional 5 min. After cooling at room-temperature, the ï¬�nal pro- ducts were puriï¬�ed by precipitation with petroleum ether three times and stored in vacuum desiccator. The Quantum yield of the ï¬‚uorescent Silane@CDs was also calculated using standard method (Lakowicz, 2006). Quinine sul- fate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as standard reference sample which has ï¬�xed and known ï¬‚uorescence quantum yield value (QYÂ¼0.54 at 360 nm). The QY of the Silane@CDs was calculated according the following equation: Ð¤ Ð¤= ( )I n I A nA / 1x x x xstd std 2 std std2 whereÐ¤ is the QY, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript â€œStdâ€� refers to the standard sample with known QY and â€œxâ€� for the Silane@CDs samples. 2.3. Synthesis of AFP imprinted polymer Firstly, the template molecule AFP (100.0 mL, dissolved in 0.5 mL of DMSO), monomers: APA (1.0 mmol, dissolved in 0.5 mL of DMSO), NIPAm (2.0 mmol, dissolved in 0.5 mL of DMSO), Sila- ne@CDs (20.0 mg, dissolved in 0.5 ml DMSO), and cross-linker N,N â€² bisacrylamide (2.0 mmol, dissolved in 0.5 mL DMSO) were mixed together. To this mixture, 1.0 mmol APS (dissolved in 0.5 mL DMSO) was added and the test tube was covered with Teï¬‚on tape and kept under the oven for 3 h at 60 Â°C. After polymerization the adduct polymer (containing template inside the matrix) was ex- tracted by 0.3 M sodium hydroxide solution and the prepared AFP- imprinted polymer was stored in desiccator. For comparison, a non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was also fabricated following the same procedure, but in the absence of template. The elemental composition of prepared MIP and NIP were also studied by ele- mental analysis method and discussed in supporting information (Section S1). 2.4. Stimuli-responsive behavior of AFP-imprinted polymer In order to evaluate the stimuli-responsive behavior of the prepared polymers, an equilibrium binding experiments was per- formed with ï¬�xed concentration of polymer (5.0 mg). The con- centration of template was varied from 3.96 to 80.0 ng mL 1. The binding experiments were performed at three different tempera- tures (i.e. 25, 35 and 45 Â°C) and three different pH values (4.0, 7.0, and 9.0) for 2.0 h to reach adsorption equilibrium. After equili- brium the polymer was removed by simple ï¬�ltration and the concentration of template remaining in the solution was measured by electrochemical method. The amount of template bonded with MIP-cavity can be determined by the difference in concentration before and after the adsorption. The adsorption capacity (Q, Î¼g/g) of the template to the polymer matrix is calculated by the fol- lowing equation: = ( âˆ’ ) ( )Q C C V 2 0 t RE TRW2.5. Selectivity study The speciï¬�c recognition property of the imprinted polymer is also evaluated by calculating the imprinting factor (IF), which is deï¬�ned as: = ( ) Q Q IF 3 MIP NIP here QMIP and QNIP are the adsorption capacities of the template and/or interfering compounds on imprinted polymer and the corresponding non-imprinted polymer, respectively. 2.6. Quantitative estimation of AFP in aqueous and real samples For quantitative analysis by ï¬‚uorescence measurement, ï¬�rstly, AFP-imprinted polymer (5.0 mg) was added to 2.0 mL distilled water and sonicated for 2 min. The ï¬‚uorescence (PL) intensity recorded for this MIP solution was used as base line. To this so- lution, various concentration of AFP was added and the mixture was stirred at 25 Â°C for 10 min to facilitate the binding of AFP onto the imprinted sites. After that, the corresponding PL intensity was recorded by the ï¬‚uorescence spectrophotometer. Contrast to the other ï¬‚uorescence sensors, herein, the intensity of MIP get en- hanced after with addition of template molecule. The ï¬‚uorescence enhancing efï¬�ciency of the imprinted polymer with respect to variation in the concentration of AFP was calculated to plot a ca- libration equation. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as three times the standard deviation for the blank measurement in the absence of template, divided by the slope of the calibration plot between PL intensity and concentration of AFP (Skoog et al.,1984). 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Characterization of silane@CDs Herein, the silane@CDs was synthesized using sucrose as a precursor compound (Scheme 1). The synthesized silane@CDs was characterized by UVâ€“vis and ï¬‚uorescence (PL) spectroscopic analysis. The UVâ€“vis absorption spectrum of silane@CDs was recorded by dispersing the silane@CDs in distilled water. As shown in the Fig. 1-A, the synthesized silane@CDs exhibited an adsorption peak at 280 nm owing to nâ€“Ï€* transition of â€“CÂ¼O and Ï€â€“Ï€* transition of â€“CÂ¼Câ€“ bonds present at the surface of silane@CDs (Yang et al., 2014). The as-prepared silane@CDs is easily soluble in ethanol and CT EDwhere C0 and Ct are the initial concentration and the residual concentration of the template, respectively (ng mL 1), V is the volume of the initial solution (mL), and W is the weight of the polymer (mg). The data of the absorption experiment was further processed with the Scatchard equation to estimate the binding parameters of the MIP and NIP (Scatchard, 1949). The Scatchard equation was described as follows: ( )= âˆ’Q C Q Q Kmax a Here, Qmax and Ka is the apparent maximum number of binding sites and equilibrium association constant of the binding sites, respectively. The values of Qmax, Ka and Kd (i.e. 1/KaÂ¼dissociationwater. Both of their suspensions in ethanol and water exhibit ï¬‚uor P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463 457Scheme 1. Graphical representation for synthesis ofstrong blue ï¬‚uorescence, as shown in Fig. 1-F. The PL intensity of silane@CDs emission spectra is highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 450 nm the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelength and the ï¬‚uorescence intensity decreased (Fig. 1-B). This excitation- dependent PL behavior was extensively reported in ï¬‚uorescent carbon-based nanomaterials and it may be explained by the in homogeneities of the orbital energy distribution of the defects states (Riggs et al., 2000). The quantum yield of 40% was found for silane@CDs monomer. In order to characterize the morphology and the size of the CDs, HR-TEM and EDAX analyses were performed. As depicted in the Fig. 1-C and D, the silane@CDs is of uniform, mono-dispersed, and spherical structures. The distribution of the silane@CDs diameter ranges from 10 to 15 nm, with an average size of 12 nm. The compositional study of silane@CDs was performed by EDAX spectra (Fig. 1-E), where three peaks was obtained for â€˜Siâ€™, â€˜Câ€™, and â€˜Oâ€™ elements, which conï¬�rms the successful formation of silane@CDs. 3.2. Bonding characterization of AFP-imprinted polymer The boding interaction between polymer matrix and template was studied by UVâ€“vis, and PL spectroscopic analyses. The UVâ€“ visible spectra for synthesized polymers and template molecule were shown in Fig. 1-A. The template molecule shows a very sharp peak at 210 nm, corresponds to the Ï€â€“Ï€n transition of â€“NÂ¼O groups present in the molecule (Corral et al., 2011) [Leyva et al., 2011]. The same peak also persist in the spectra of adduct polymer, along with one additional peak at the higher wavelength (275 nm). The presence of silane@CDs peak at 275 nm conï¬�rms the successfully incorporation of CDs in the polymer matrix. After template extraction from the adduct polymer the ï¬�rst peak RE TR Aescence, stimuli-responsive AFP-imprinted polymer.C TE Ddisappears from the spectra, suggest the complete extraction of template molecule. However, when AFP is further spiked in the MIP solution the peak re-appears. The study conï¬�rms the suc- cessful incorporation of CDs in the polymer matrix, creation of speciï¬�c AFP cavity in the polymer matrix and template-rebinding. The similar results were also observed from PL study. The prepared adduct polymer showed two symmetric ï¬‚uorescence emission peaks at 325 and 425 nmwhen excited at 290 nm (Fig. 2- A). The peak observed at 425 nm was in accordance with the si- lane@CDs described above. After template extraction from adduct polymer, the PL intensity decreases to some extent, which suggest that binding of template to the polymer matrix did not quench the PL intensity, however, enhances the PL intensity. This was further conï¬�rmed by addition of template in the MIP solution, where a sharp increase in PL intensity of polymer was observed, due to template-MIP bonding interaction (Fig. S3-A). 3.3. Characterization AFP-imprinted polymer Thermo-sensitive polymers can change the dimensions of their structures depending on temperature. Usually, at low tempera- tures, the hydrogen bond interactions formed between polymer chains and water molecules predominate, which is responsible for swelling of polymer and their water solubility (Xu et al., 2013). When the temperature is increased higher than the low-critical solution temperature (LCST), the hydrogen bonds are destroyed and hydrophobic interactions increase, which results in the ag- gregation of polymer chains and contraction of the polymer net- work. Herein, to calculate the LCST of reported MIP and NIP, the UV-visible analysis was performed (Fig. 2-B). As shown in the ï¬�gure, with increases in temperature from 20 Â°C to 50 Â°C the ab- sorbance increases, but beyond 35 Â°C a sharp change was ob- served. Therefore, 35 Â°C temperature was taken as LCST of the P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463458proposed MIP and/or NIP. In addition, dynamic layer light scattering study was carried out to characterize the stimuli-responsive changes in size of MIPs in aqueous dispersion at different temperatures (35 and 45 Â°C) and pH values (4.0 and 5.5) (Fig. 3-A and B). At 35 Â°C, the diameter is in the range of 450â€“480 nm for NIP and 460â€“490 nm for MIP, with an average value of 455 and 465 nm, for NIP and MIP, respectively. Upon heating to 45 Â°C, the diameter of both polymers get Fig. 1. (A) UVâ€“visible spectra of MIP, adduct, template, silane@CDs and MIP rebounded excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 450 nm) of silane@CDs. HR-TEM images of sila (F) Camera picture of silane@CDs suspensions in ethanol and water exhibiting strong b RE TR Adecreased and reached to 376.10 and 378.0 nm. The decrease in polymer diameter at elevated temperature can be ascribed to the collapse of polymers upon heating. The similar behavior was also obtained, when the DLS study was performed at different pH va- lues. The corresponding curve was shown in the Fig. 3-C. As de- picted, at low pH (4.0) the size of polymer is 310 nm, which increases with increase in pH value and maximum were obtained at pHÂ¼5.5 (diameterÂ¼450 nm). At low pH, carboxyl groups are with template, (B) photoluminescence emission spectra (with progressively longer ne@CDs at low (C) and high (D) magniï¬�cations. (E) EDAX spectra of silane@CDs. lue ï¬‚uorescence in UV light. CT ED ers. spec P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463 459protonated and hydrophobic interactions dominate, leading to volume shrinkage. At high pH, carboxyl groups dissociate into carboxylate ions, resulting in a high charge density in the polymer and causing it to swell (Xu et al., 2013). Herein, we have optimized pH 5.5 and 35 Â°C temperature, to study the responsive behavior of polymer during template binding and extraction. The morphology of the imprinted (MIP) and non-imprinted (NIP) polymers were assessed by the FE-SEM analysis and the recorded FE-SEM images were shown in (Fig. 2-Câ€“F). The NIP Fig. 2. (A) photoluminescence emission spectra of MIP, MIPÃ¾AFP, and adduct polym SEM images of MIP (C and D) and NIP (E and F) at low and high magniï¬�cations, re A surface had a smooth, uniform, compact morphology, whereas, MIP surface had a comparatively porous surface at low magniï¬�- cation (Fig. 2-C and D). When the magniï¬�cation was increased, some small nanoparticles were also visualized on the MIP surface, which is not visible in the NIP surface, at similar magniï¬�cation (Fig. 2-E and F). The absence of pores or cavities on NIP and pre- sence in the MIP, suggests that the porosity generated in the MIP surface is due to template extraction. The elemental composition of MIP/NIP was also calculated and found as: theoretical (%): CÂ¼41.13, NÂ¼7.99; experimental (%): CÂ¼41.0, HÂ¼5.21, NÂ¼8.03. 3.4. Binding isotherm and Scatchard analysis A binding isotherm is a measure of the concentration depen- dent recognition behavior of a system. The adsorption capacity of polymer (MIP and/or NIP) for AFP was, ï¬�rstly, increases with in- crease in initial concentration of template molecule (AFP), and then become constant thereafter, due to the saturation of re- cognition sites. The imprinting factor (IF) of the MIPs was also found satisfactory (Fig. 3-D). The amount of AFP bonded on the MIP cavities was larger than that on the NIPs at all the con- centrations studied, which demonstrated that the protein im- printing was effective in constructing AFP recognition sites. Fig. S4 shows the Scatchard plots of the MIP and NIP in the presence of AFP. It shows a linear relation between Q/C and Q with the linear regression equation: = ( âˆ’ Â± ) + ( Â± ) = =Q C n R/ 0.076 0.002 Q 15.226 0.121 , 10, 0.99.2 According to the slope and intercept of this linear equation, the RE RKa, Qmax and Kd (1/Ka) were calculated to be 0.076 L mg 1, 200.34 m g g 1, and 13.15 mg L 1, respectively. This suggests the homo- geneous and uniform binding sites were formed in AFP-imprinted polymer. However, in NIP, no such linear relation was observed which suggest the absence of AFP-speciï¬�c binding sites in the non- imprinted polymer. 3.5. Stimuli-responsive binding of the template to MIP/NIP (B) Cloud point measurement of MIP and NIP for calculation of their LCST values. FE- tively.CT EDThe effects of temperature on the adsorption capacity were investigated for both imprinted and non-imprinted polymers at different temperatures (25, 35, 40 and 45 Â°C) and pH values (4.0, 5.5, 7.0 and 9.0). Fig. 3-E shows the adsorption capacities of MIP and NIPs for template molecule (AFP) at various temperatures. As observed, the adsorption capacity of MIPs was maximum at 35 Â°C and much larger than those at 45 Â°C for MIPs. The similar trend was followed by NIP also, with smaller adsorption capacity, due to absence of speciï¬�c cavities inside the polymer matrix. From the results, we can conclude the temperature responsive behavior of the prepared polymers which become hydrophobic and con- tractive, above the LCST, due to loss of hydrogen bonding, resulting in closing of pore and/or cavities and therefore poor adsorption capacity was observed. Meanwhile, the inï¬‚uence of different pH values on the binding capacities of MIP and NIP was also studied (Fig. 3-F). The similar behavior of polymers was observed in this case also. Herein, in the acidic pH (i.e. 4.0 and 5.5) the adsorption capacities are much higher than the neutral and alkaline medium. Non-imprinted polymers also show the same trend of adsorption capacity with lesser value than imprinted polymers. Therefore, it can be con- cluded that the polymers shows a very high effect of temperature and pH during the template binding with the imprinted cavities. Before the LCST or optimum pH value, the relatively loose struc- ture of the imprinted sites restricts the recognition ability to attain its maximum (Xu et al., 2013). And when temperature increased higher than LCST or the pH reached beyond optimum value, the binding ability decreased sharply; this could be attributed to the structural shrinkage of the imprinted sites. Both tight and loose P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463460spatial structures of the imprinted sites led to the decrease in the recognition ability. 3.6. Logic operation of AFP-imprinted polymer with temperature and pH as the inputs Herein, we have tried to the â€œORâ€� logic gate functioning with temperature and pH as the inputs based on a general polymeric design. Fig. 4-A and truth Table (Fig. 4-C) shows the ï¬‚uorescence intensities of AFP-imprinted polymer (MIP) in the presence of template molecule at different temperature and pH. It was already discussed in the last paragraph, that polymer shows maximum binding capacity for template molecule at LCST and optimum pH. Therefore, the optimum pH i.e. 5.5 and LCST i.e. 35 Â°C have taken Fig. 3. Size distribution curve for NIP and MIP obtained by DLS study at 35 Â°C (A), 45 Â°C factor for MIP and NIP with different concentrations of AFP, where the imprinting fac different temperature and (F) pH values with corresponding imprinting factors.RE TR Aas inputs for OR gate and the corresponding PL intensity has taken as output. In brief, the change in pH and temperature of the medium plays a key role in the off-on switching of the ï¬‚uorescent molecular OR gate (The camera pictures showing role of inputs were given in supporting information as Fig. S3). To indicate clearly the function of the imprinted polymer as a logic gate, the four situations (Iâ€“IV in Fig. 4-C) are examined, in which tem- perature (Input 1) is 35 Â°C (high, binary 1) or 20 Â°C (low, 0) and pH (Input 2) is 5.5 (high, 1) or 4 (low, 0). As shown in Fig. 4-A and C, the ï¬‚uorescence intensity (Output) of the imprinted polymer in the presence of template molecule is distinctly high (1) only when either of the Input (1 or 2) is (1, 1). In contrast, the ï¬‚uorescence intensity holds a low level when (Input 1, Input 2) is (0, 0). The ï¬‚uorescence intensity at Output (1,1; 1,0; and 0,1) is over 6-fold (B) and (C) different pH values (4.0 and 5.5). (D) Binding isotherm and imprinting tor (IF) was deï¬�ned as IFÂ¼QMIP/QNIP. (E) Adsorption capacities of MIP and NIP at CT ED P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463 461that at Output (0,0). It is also important to mention that the change in pH and temperature does not alter the PL intensity of polymer in the absence of template molecule. These results show that the AFP-imprinted polymer performs the OR logic operation with the two inputs, temperature and pH. 3.7. Quantitative estimation of AFP in aqueous and real samples To explore the real world application of as-prepared AFP-im- printed polymer, the quantitative estimation of AFP was per- formed in the aqueous as well as real samples (human blood serum and blood plasma). Firstly, the protein was detected in the Fig. 4. (A) â€œORâ€� logic gate functioning with temperature and pH as the inputs based on normal temperature), (d) MIPÃ¾AFP (at pHÂ¼5.5), (e) MIPÃ¾AFP (at 35 Â°C), (f) MIPÃ¾AFP (a range of 3.96 ng mL 1 (1) to 85.0 ng mL 1 (8)] on the PL intensity of AFP imprinted polym the PL intensity, during AFP estimation with imprinting factor.RE TR Aknown concentration solution of analyte, where, in contrast to the quenching of ï¬‚uorescence property in the presence of template, herein, the ï¬‚uorescence intensity of the AFP-imprinted polymer turned out to increase sensitively in the presence of AFP molecule (Fig. S3-A). Fig. 4-B revealed the effect of AFP concentration on the PL intensity of AFP-IP. A representation of the lower concentration range of 3.96 ng mL 1 revealed linearity up to 80.0 ng mL 1 of template concentration, with a R2Â¼0.992. The linear calibration equation is given below: Î”IÂ¼(Iâ€“I0)Â¼(0.71729.7)CÃ¾(102.071.19), nÂ¼10, R2Â¼0.993, LODÂ¼0.42 ng mL 1, where, I0Â¼PL Intensity of MIP solution and IÂ¼PL Intensity of MIP, after binding with different concentration of a general polymeric design: (a) MIP, (b) MIPÃ¾AFP (at normal pH), (c) MIPÃ¾AFP (at t pHÂ¼5.5 and 35 Â°C), (B) PL spectra showing the effect of AFP concentration [in the er, (C) truth Table for determination of AFP, (D) effect of interfering compounds on CT ED MIP and NIPs. However, when binary mixture of AFP and inter- fering compounds was studies, 100% response for AFP was ob- P. Karfa et al. / Biosensors and Bioelectronics 78 (2016) 454â€“463462 A template molecule. With further increase in the concentration (85.0 ng mL 1), the PL intensity remain constant, suggest the sa- turation of binding sites (Fig. 4-B). Furthermore, to investigate the practical application of the proposed sensor, the MIP was applied in the blood serum, blood plasma and urine samples for AFP. Firstly, 1.0 mL of human blood serum, plasma and/or urine sample was taken in the vial, in which 2.0 mL of MIP solution (5.0 mg) was added. The corresponding PL intensity was recorded followed by spiking of known concentra- tion of AFP. The corresponding results were portrayed in the Ta- bles 1 and S1. As can be seen in Table 1, satisfactory recoveries for Table 1 Estimation of AFP in human real samples using molecularly imprinted polymer as ï¬‚uorescence sensor. S. N. Sample Added (ng mL 1) Determined va- lue7S.D. (ng mL 1) Recovery (%) RSD (%) 1. Blood plasma â€“ 4.7870.05 â€“ 1.0 Spike 4.78a 4.8070.05 100.4 1.0 Total 9.56 9.5170.10 99.4 1.0 Spike 13.8 13.6070.17 98.5 1.2 Total 23.36 23.3670.25 100.0 1.0 2. Blood serum â€“ 4.8070.06 â€“ 1.2 Spike 5.86a 5.90 70.10 100.6 1.6 Total 10.66 10.5070.11 98.4 1.0 Spike 19.20 18.8070.20 97.9 1.0 Total 29.86 29.8670.30 100.0 1.0 3. Urine â€“ Nil â€“ â€“ Spike 5.00 4.9970.05 99.8 1.0 Total 5.00 5.0070.05 100.0 1.0 Spike 15.0a 15.1070.18 100.6 1.1 Total 20.0 19.9070.23 99.5 1.1 a The same concentration of AFP was also analyzed using commercially avail- able ELISA kit and almost similar results were obtained.all samples were obtained between 98.5% and 100.6% with RSDs of 1.0â€“1.6%. The values meet with the requirement of trace analysis, and the method can reach the required detectability for AFP in bio- medical diagnosis as a sensitive, selective, rapid and cost-effective sensor. The analytical validation and application potential of the pro- posed method were evaluated by comparing the assay results of real sample analysis with the standard and commercially available enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). Each human serum/ plasma samples were analyzed for three times and the relative deviation between these two methods was from 1.5% to 1.0% (Table 1). Almost similar results were obtained from ELISA and the proposed method, showing the applicability of the proposed sensor in real biological samples. Analytical performances of the developed methods for detection of AFP were also compared with other MIP-based and immuno-sensors and discussed in support- ing information as Section S2 and Table S2. 3.8. Cross-reactivity study Selectivity of a sensor plays a very important role in analyzing biological samples, which consist of several potential inter- ferences, including amino acids (Lys and His), proteins (Glo, Alb, IgG, A1 and Bil), metal ions (NaÃ¾ , KÃ¾), and other compounds (urea, UA, Glu and AA). The effect of these interfering compounds on AFP detection was investigated and portrayed in Fig. 4-D. The study was performed on both imprinted and non-imprinted polymers in the absence (only interfering compounds) and Authors are thankful to Department of Science and Technology, RE TRGovernment of India for sanction of the Fast Track Research Project for Young Scientists to Dr. Rashmi Madhuri (Ref. no.: SB/FT/CS-155/ 2012) and Dr. Prashant K. Sharma (Ref. no.: SR/FTP/PS-157/2011). Dr. Sharma (FRS/34/2012-2013/APH) and Dr. Madhuri (FRS/43/ 2013-2014/AC) are also thankful to Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad for Grant of Major Research Project under the Faculty Research Scheme. We are also thankful to Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS), Department of Atomic Energy, Govern- ment of India for major research project (34/14/21/2014-BRNS). D. K. thankful to University Grant Commission for providing post- doctoral fellowship (Dr. D.S. Kothari) (F.4-2/2006 (BSR)/13-899/ 2013). Appendix A. Supplementary material Supplementary data associated with this article can be found inIn summary, we have demonstrated that molecular imprinting may be used to synthesize polymers that exhibit â€˜ORâ€™ logic-gate based binding properties for predetermined input species. The selectivity of this polymeric logic gates can be customized simply by the choice of inputs (pH or temperature). The stimuli-re- sponsive MIP for AFP was synthesized using three different func- tional monomers i.e. NIPAm, Tyrosine-derivative and Silane@CDs. The silane@CDs was used to add the ï¬‚uorescence property in the imprinted polymer, which get enhanced after binding with dif- ferent concentrations of template molecules. The resulting PL in- tensity could be adjusted by change in the pH or temperature as external stimulus. The proposed imprinted polymer based ï¬‚uor- escence sensor was successfully applied for detection of AFP in real samples (human blood, plasma and urine). The results obtained by the proposed sensor were also compared with the commercially available ELISA kit for AFP detection and shows almost similar results. Therefore, the sensor could be used as a better replace- ment of costly detection techniques for the detection of AFP as heptacellular cancer biomarker. Acknowledgment CT Etained in the presence of these interfering compounds. The re- sponse for template and the speciï¬�c recognition property of the imprinted polymer is also evaluated in terms of imprinting factor (IF) and the results were shown in the Fig. 4-D. This shows that there is no effect of interfering elements during the AFP estimation using imprinted polymer as sensor. From the above observations, it could be concluded that the AFP cavities present in the polymer matrix can able to distinguish target molecules from others through their size, shape and functional group distribution. The reproducibility, regeneration and stability of proposed MIP-sensor was also studied and discussed in supporting information as Sec- tion S3 and Fig. S5. 4. Conclusion Dpresence (binary mixture of AFP and interfering compounds) of template molecule. It was found that almost negligible change in the PL intensity was observed for interfering compounds at boththe online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2015.11.092. This shows that there is no effect of interfering elements during the AFP estimation using imprinted polymer as sensor. From the above observations, it could be concluded that the AFP cavities present in the polymer matrix can able to distinguish target molecules from others through their size, shape and functional group distribution. The reproducibility, regeneration and stability of proposed MIP-sensor was also studied and discussed in supporting information as Sec- tion S3 and Fig. S5. 4. head.ce:title: RETRACTED: A fluorescent molecularly-imprinted polymer gate with temperature and pH as inputs for detection of alpha-fetoprotein

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, Indian School of Mines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solid State and Structural Chemistry Unit, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Solid State and Structural Chemistry Unit, Indian Institute of Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Nanomaterials Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Physics, Indian School of Mines, Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Nanomaterials Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Physics, Indian School of Mines

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article has been retracted: please see Elsevier Policy on Article Withdrawal (https://www.elsevier.com/about/our-business/policies/article-withdrawal).
This article has been retracted at the request of the Editor following concerns raised by a reader.
The article reuses an electron micrograph from a previous publication while claiming that these are different particles.
Fig. 1C/D were reused from Fig. 2B published in Biosensors and Bioelectronics, Volume 73, 15 November 2015, Pages 234–244, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2015.06.005.
This problem with the data presented casts doubt on all the data, and accordingly also the conclusions based on that data, in this publication.

As such this article represents a severe abuse of the scientific publishing system. The scientific community takes a very strong view on this matter and apologies are offered to readers of the journal that this was not detected during the submission process.
